title: Ben Shapiro Calls ‘The Odyssey’ a ‘Masterpiece’ Despite Conservative Outrage: Christopher Nolan Is the ‘Best Working Director Today’
description: Ben Shapiro posted an 18-minute review of Christopher Nolan’s “The Odyssey” to his YouTube channel on Saturday, in which he called the Greek epic a
body:
Ben Shapiro posted an 18-minute review of Christopher Nolan’s “The Odyssey” to his YouTube channel on Saturday, in which he called the Greek epic a “masterpiece” despite the conservative outrage about the film’s cast and historical accuracy.

	“Nolan is unparalleled. He’s the best director working today, without a doubt. He just creates iconic movie after iconic movie,” Shapiro said. He also had lots of praise for star Matt Damon, adding, “I think he’s great in nearly everything. He sort of disappears into the part in a way that he doesn’t in a lot of other movies. He’s great as Odysseus.”
	

	He likewise gushed about several other members of the supporting cast: “Robert Pattinson as Antinous is an excellent antagonist. He’s sleazy and gross. Tom Holland as Telemachus is excellent, well cast. Anne Hathaway does a great job as Penelope.”


	Shapiro also fired back at those who complained about Lupita Nyong’o playing Helen of Troy.

	“So, I know a lot of people were like, ‘Oh my god, Lupita Nyong’o is supposed to look like Diane Kruger.’ I don’t know. Diane Krueger doesn’t look very Greek. She’s German,” Shapiro said. “Lupita Nyong’o is a beautiful woman. But here’s the thing, actually, in the script, Nolan goes out of his way to explain that the war wasn’t really launched over Helen being gorgeous.”

	The political commentator did, however, take issue with Elliot Page’s casting as Sinon. He called Page’s role an “absolute puzzler” before pointing out that he is “about 5’2 and 105 pounds soaking wet and has the voice of a woman. It doesn’t work.”
	

	Despite outrage from right-wing voices like Elon Musk, Nolan recently told The Telegraph that he’s unbothered by the noise. He explained that online backlash “comes with the territory” when making a blockbuster.

	“Remember, I spent 10 years of my life dealing with Batman,” Nolan explained. “When I came on ‘Begins,’ writers and artists had been working on this beloved character for almost 65 years, and a lot of freighted thoughts were out there about what he represents. And what I learnt over my time on that trilogy is you can’t worry about any of that at all. What you have to do is honor the original text by interpreting it in the strongest way you personally can.”

schema.headline: Ben Shapiro Calls ‘The Odyssey’ a ‘Masterpiece’ Despite Conservative Outrage: Christopher Nolan Is the ‘Best Working Director Today’

title: Venice Critics’ Week Lineup: Shalini Adnani’s Family Drama ‘Our Share of Sand,’ AI-Assisted Colombian Doc ‘The End of Times’ and More
description: London-based Chilean-Indian director Shalini Adnani’s debut feature “Our Share of Sand,” about a 12-year-old girl named Maya who returns to rural In
body:
London-based Chilean-Indian director Shalini Adnani’s debut feature “Our Share of Sand,” about a 12-year-old girl named Maya who returns to rural India to reconnect with her estranged father, is among standout entries in the upcoming Venice Film Festival Critics’ Week.

	The section, dedicated to first works from around the world, also comprises two titles from Colombia: the fiction-documentary hybrid “The End of Times,” a political piece co-directed by Bibiana Rojas Gómez and Juan David Cárdenas that uses artificial intelligence and explores colonialism and capitalism in Colombian history, and Sebastián Múnera’s drama “Meteorite,” about plans to build an amusement parkon the grounds of the former estate of drug lord Pablo Escobar.
	

	Italy also features prominently in the lineup, with Paris-based Tommaso Usberti’s “Before the War,” about a young man named Guido raised within a Northern Italian racist milieu who falls in love with a Kosovar immigrant named Sonia, ensconced in a competition slot.


	Also from Italy is the section’s out-of-competition opener, existential noir “Giaffa the Great” directed by Marco Santi, while the closer — also out-of competition — is Italian rap musical “Rider,” co-directed by Roan Johnson and the local music producer known as Dade (Davide Pavanello). “Rider” follows a young food delivery driver who delivers a huge sushi order to a luxurious villa on the outskirts of Milan and is invited to stay for the evening.

	The seven features competing in Venice Critics’ Week are all by first-time directors and all world premieres.
	

	Rounding off the section’s competition are Tunisian dystopian drama “The H@llow Man” byKamel Laaridhi, set in 2059 Tunisia where a biochip regulates memory and suppresses emotion; Chinese drama “Zoom In, Zoom Out,” by Dong Jie, about a young video game designer who embarks on a journey to seek his missing roommate Gao following a clue hidden in an online game; and Brazilian drama “London,” by Victor Di Marco and Márcio Picoli, about a dilemma faced by a disabled young adult.

	“Whilst the industry at large is grappling with its crises, these works are proof that cinema never stopped asserting its own cruciality,” said Venice Critics’ Week general delegate Beatrice Fiorentino in a statement. “This cinema is fierce and independent, daring and vibrant, resolute in its reminder that our collective imagination can, and indeed must, continue to be rewritten.”

	All Venice Critics’ Week competition entries will compete alongside titles in the official selection for the fest’s Lion of the Future prize, which is worth $100,000.

	The 83rd edition of Venice Film Festival will run Sept. 2-12, with its official selection lineup being announced on Thursday. See all the Venice Critics’ Week titles below.

	COMPETITION 

	“The End of Times,” Bibiana Rojas Gómez, Juan David Cárdenas (Colombia)

	“The H@llow Man,” Kamel Laaridhi (Tunisia)

	“London,” Victor Di Marco, Márcio Picoli (Brazil)

	“Meteorite,” Sebastián Múnera (Colombia)

	“Our Share of Sand,” Shalini Adnani (India, United Kingdom, Greece)

	“Prima Della Guerra,” Tommaso Usberti (France, Italy)

	“Zoom In, Zoom Out,” Dong Jie (China)

	OUT OF COMPETITION

	“Il Grande Giaffa,” Marco Santi (Italy) — Opening Film

	“Rider,” Roan Johnson, Davide Pavanello (Italy) — Closing Film

schema.headline: Venice Critics’ Week Lineup: Shalini Adnani’s Family Drama ‘Our Share of Sand,’ AI-Assisted Colombian Doc ‘The End of Times’ and More

title: ‘New-Gen’ Universe Launches First Consumer Products Line With Comic-Con Trading Cards (EXCLUSIVE)
description: New-Gen Universe, Inc. is launching its first consumer products initiative at San Diego Comic-Con, debuting an exclusive collector’s trading card se
body:
New-Gen Universe, Inc. is launching its first consumer products initiative at San Diego Comic-Con, debuting an exclusive collector’s trading card set made with Cryptozoic Entertainment to celebrate its Marvel-distributed comic series.

	Just 300 convention exclusive boxes exist worldwide for the July 23–26 event, making this among the rarest “New-Gen” collectibles released to date. Designed solely for the convention, the set also builds momentum for the upcoming 3D CGI animated series “New-Gen Nanobot Heroes.”
	

	In the comic, twin brothers Sean and Chris learn their bodies have been enhanced by nanotechnology, thrusting them into a fight against the villain Deadalus within a futuristic world where nature and technology exist side by side.


	“New-Gen” was created by J.D. Matonti, Julia Coppola and Chris Matonti and has grown from its Marvel-distributed comic book origins into a transmedia franchise spanning publishing, animation and consumer products. The Comic-Con cards give fans a tangible link between the source comics and the animated series still to come.

	The initiative is led by Debra Joester, president of Joester Loria Group, who is spearheading New-Gen’s expanding consumer products program.

	“We are thrilled to collaborate with Debra Joester and Cryptozoic Entertainment on this first New-Gen consumer products initiative. We envision many more imaginative opportunities like these as we work with Joester Loria Group, Toonz Media Group, and world class talent to bring our New-Gen Nanobot Heroes story to children of all ages,” said J.D. Matonti, creator of “New-Gen.”
	

	Each box holds a set of 13 collectible cards: 11 base cards drawn from artwork in the comic book series, a single rainbow foil card, and one autograph card bearing the signature of either Matonti, voice actor Molly Fahey or voice actor Tony Oliver. Every autograph card carries an on-card signature and a limited run of 100, and select base and foil cards were produced as unique one-of-one pieces.

	Buyers at the convention will also receive a free “New-Gen” graphic novel, with a foreword penned by Mark Hamill, that traces the franchise’s origin story.

	“We are delighted to partner with J.D. Matonti, Toonz Media Group, and the extraordinary talent behind New-Gen Nanobot Heroes,” said Debra Joester. “Fans are craving the next evolution of superheroes and J.D. has created the gripping storytelling, compelling characters and rich, imaginative new world that delivers just that.”

	Attendees can also preview new artwork for “New-Gen Nanobot Heroes” ahead of its animated debut on the franchise’s website, which will show the heroes touching down by spaceship in the series’ utopian setting. A separate giveaway offers fans the chance to win a graphic novel signed by Hamill.

	The card release also builds momentum for “New-Gen Nanobot Heroes,” the coming 13-episode, 22-minute 3D CGI series from writer-director Matonti, whose representation includes Evan Anglin and Eric Williams at Zero Gravity Management. New-Gen Universe and Toonz Media Group are producing the show, with Chris Matonti, Julia Coppola, Brad Heisman and J.D. Matonti serving as executive producers.

	Writers on the project include Brent Friedman, known for “Star Wars: The Clone Wars,” and Eugene Son, who worked on “Avengers Assemble,” alongside four other writers. Toonz Media Group will distribute the series internationally, and Ketchup Entertainment will handle its domestic release.

	Voice talent set to appear includes Finn Wolfhard, Nick Wolfhard, Anya Chalotra, Luke Wilson, Henry Rollins, Taylor Gray, Molly Fahey, Lena Headey and Tony Oliver, and John Loeffler composed the score.

	New-Gen Universe is a transmedia company built around the New-Gen IP and other properties; its advisory board counts New-Gen’s own creators alongside former Marvel CFO Ken West and Bonnie Arnold, a producer on “How to Train Your Dragon.” Co-producer Toonz Media Group has spent upward of 25 years in the animation business, turning out an estimated 5,000 minutes annually of CGI and 2D programming, with past credits on “Wolverine and the X-Men” for Marvel, “Speed Racer: The Next Generation” for Lionsgate and “Mostly Ghostly” for Universal.

schema.headline: ‘New-Gen’ Universe Launches First Consumer Products Line With Comic-Con Trading Cards (EXCLUSIVE)

title: Future Debuts at No. 1 With ‘The Real Me,’ as Rolling Stones and ‘Heated Rivalry’ Soundtrack Also Bow in Top 10
description: We have seen the future of who’s moving up in the list of artists contending to have the most No. 1 albums ever, and its name is Future. The rapper
body:
We have seen the future of who’s moving up in the list of artists contending to have the most No. 1 albums ever, and its name is Future. The rapper debuted on top of the Billboard 200 with “The Real Me,” landing his 12th No. 1 on the chart. That means he’s now in fifth place, by himself, in the ranking of all-time chart-topping album artists.

	“The Real Me” debuted with 131,000 equivalent album units to easily slip in ahead of Olivia Rodrigo’s “You Look Pretty Sad for a Girl So in Love,” which moved another 96,000 units to hold on at No. 2 in its fifth week on the chart.
	

	Previously, on the list of artists with the most No. 1 albums, Future had been tied with Eminem, with 11. With this 12th No. 1, he leaves Eminem behind to hold fifth place all by himself. The four artists ahead of him on that esteemed list are the Beatles (with 19 No. 1s), Drake and Taylor Swift (tied for second with 15 each) and Jay-Z (with 14).


	Two albums besides Future’s debuted in the top 10. The Rolling Stones‘ “Foreign Tongues” bowed at No. 7 with 53,000 equivalent album units. That’s roughly in the same ballpark as a recent album by the Stones’ contemporary, Paul McCartney, which debuted with 63,000 in June.

	The remaining top 10 entry was the soundtrack for television’s “Heated Rivalry,” which premiered in the No. 9 slot with 41,000 units. Billboard reported that this marked the first time a TV soundtrack had bowed in the top 10 in more than a decade — specifically, since the “Empire” soundtrack did it in 2015. (One other TV soundtrack, “Hazbin Hotel,” the companion album for an animated show, did reach the top 10 last year, but only after debuting at No. 70.)
	

	Future’s figures for “The Real Me,” broken down, show streaming numbers far outstripping sales, which is typical for hip-hop. The album sold only 13,000 copies, but had 120.29 million on-demand streams, which translated into 118,000 SEA units.

	Of course, the Stones’ numbers skewed the opposite way, with 48,500 albums sold, augmented by 4.48 million on-demand streams, amounting to 4,000 SEA units.

	Curiously, the “Heated Rivalry” numbers came down entirely to sales, with 41,000 copies sold on CD and vinyl and 41,000 units altogether. That’s because the score album already came out digitally back in January, but it never accrued enough units on streaming alone to impact the chart prior to the July physical release.

	Looking at the top 10, one big name is MIA: Madonna’s “Confessions II” has dropped out, after debuting last week at No. 1. Its chart position in week 2 isn’t yet known, as Luminate and Billboard only release the list of the top 10 albums on Sunday, so news of exactly where “Confessions II” came in in its sophomore session will have to wait till Monday.

	The rest of the top 10 is rounded out by Morgan Wallen’s “I’m the Problem” at No. 3, Ella Langley at No. 4, Noah Kahan at No. 5, Drake at No. 6, Michael Jackson’s “Thriller” at No. 8 and Wallen’s “One Thing at a Time” at No. 10.

schema.headline: Future Debuts at No. 1 With ‘The Real Me,’ as Rolling Stones and ‘Heated Rivalry’ Soundtrack Also Bow in Top 10

title: China Box Office: ‘Kung Fu Soccer’ Maintains Dominance as Animated ‘All Wishes Come True!’ Debuts in Second
description: Shenzhen Film Studio’s sports comedy “Kung Fu Soccer” comfortably retained the top spot at the mainland China box office during the July 17–19 weeke
body:
Shenzhen Film Studio’s sports comedy “Kung Fu Soccer” comfortably retained the top spot at the mainland China box office during the July 17–19 weekend, generating RMB501.1 million ($73.7 million) in its second weekend of release, according to data from Artisan Gateway. 

	Directed by Stephen Chow, the hit has reached a cumulative total of $214.3 million in its first nine days. The narrative marks a return to Chow’s signature athletic slapstick, following an amateur women’s team as they combine gravity-defying martial arts with professional soccer to storm a major championship tournament.
	

	“Kung Fu Soccer” was the second highest grossing film in the world over the weekend. Christopher Nolan’s “The Odyssey,” the No. 1 film in the world by some distance, will open in China Aug. 15.


	Pearl Studio’s computer-animated fantasy feature “All Wishes Come True!” debuted in second place, capturing $30.6 million over the three-day weekend frame. Directed by Mu Zhengyang, the co-production has amassed a total of $39.6 million since launching on July 18. The family-friendly mythological comedy follows eight ordinary mortals who must use their collective wit and courage to outsmart formidable deities, ultimately earning the legendary title of the Eight Immortals.

	Enlight Pictures’ adaptation of 2015 U.K. film “Miss You Already,” “A Part of Me,” opened in third position, pulling in $13.6 million during its debut frame. Directed by Ma Yingxin and starring Zhang Ruonan and Jolin Jin, the character-driven film spans a multi-decade timeline, chronicling the emotional complexities, personal growth, and shifting bonds of a deeply resilient friendship that crosses 20 years.
	

	Universal’s “Minions & Monsters” moved to fourth place in its third week of release, bringing in $3.7 million. The film has now pushed its cumulative Chinese box office haul to $40.3 million since its July 3 debut.

	Rounding out the top five, a theatrical release of Christopher Smith’s 2009 psychological horror film “Triangle” drew $3.7 million over the weekend frame. Funded by the erstwhile U.K. Film Council, the mind-bending thriller has taken $3.9 million to date. 

	Mainland China’s overall weekend grosses reached $132.9 million, while the 2026 year-to-date revenue stands at $2.97 billion, down 35.3% from the same period in 2025.

schema.headline: China Box Office: ‘Kung Fu Soccer’ Maintains Dominance as Animated ‘All Wishes Come True!’ Debuts in Second

title: James Gray to Be Honored With Locarno Film Festival Career Award
description: The Locarno Film Festival will honor James Gray with a lifetime career award at this year’s edition, where the U.S. auteur’s most recent work, “Pape
body:
The Locarno Film Festival will honor James Gray with a lifetime career award at this year’s edition, where the U.S. auteur’s most recent work, “Paper Tiger,” will screen.

	Gray will receive the Pardo alla Carriera award on Aug. 9 and will present “Paper Tiger” — which launched in May from Cannes — at the fest’s Piazza Grande outdoor venue. Set in 1986, the film follows two brothers, Irwin (Miles Teller) and Gary Pearl (Adam Driver). Irwin becomes entangled with the Russian mob after embarking on a get-rich-quick scheme to clean up the Gowanus Canal, and his former cop brother, Gary, is the only one who can get him out of trouble. “Paper Tiger” will have its North American premiere as the opening night film of the 64th New York Film Festival on Sept. 25.
	

	Two other titles directed by Gray, “We Own the Night” (2007) and “Two Lovers” (2008), will also screen at Locarno as part of the tribute.


	“Since winning the Silver Lion at Venice for ‘Little Odessa’ (1994) at age 25, James Gray has forged a singular career rooted, with piercing realism, in his upbringing in New York’s Russian-Jewish community,” the fest said in a statement, citing Gray’s “The Yards” (2000), “We Own the Night” (2007), “Two Lovers” (2008) and “The Immigrant” (2013), which cemented Gray’s standing “as one of American cinema’s most artistically distinctive voices — a filmmaker as concerned with criminal underworlds, rendered with exacting detail, as with meticulous family portraiture, often situated within the Brighton Beach of his youth and shaped by a dialogue with classic American and European auteur cinema.”
	

	The Locarno statement continued: “As he has established himself at the top tier of filmmakers in the United States, Gray’s explorations of the often elusive promise of the American dream have, over the past decade, expanded into more varied thematic terrain — colonial exploration, space travel, racial prejudice — that reshaped the popular understanding of his work while remaining distinctively his own: ‘The Lost City of Z’ (2016) with Charlie Hunnam and Robert Pattinson, ‘Ad Astra’ (2019) with Brad Pitt and ‘Armageddon Time’ (2022) with Anthony Hopkins.”

	Locarno artistic director Giona A. Nazzaro added, “James Gray is the last great novelist of American cinema. His films form a rich body of work that echoes the Jewish diaspora, film noir and Russian as well as North American literature.” He called Gray “a filmmaker with a humanist sensibility and profound cinephile knowledge” who “has experimented with genres such as science fiction and adventure while retaining his own artistic identity and voice.”

	Previous winners of the Locarno Pardo alla Carriera Award include Francesco Rosi, Bruno Ganz, Claudia Cardinale, Johnnie To, Harry Belafonte, Jane Birkin, Costa-Gavras, Tsai Ming-liang, Shah Rukh Khan and Jackie Chan.

	The 79th edition of Locarno will run Aug. 5-15.

schema.headline: James Gray to Be Honored With Locarno Film Festival Career Award

title: ‘Shark Tank India’ Sets Season 6 With Parrot Campaign on SonyLIV (EXCLUSIVE)
description: “Shark Tank India” has unveiled “#DontBeATota,” a campaign taking aim at founders who mistake the language of entrepreneurship for the substance of
body:
“Shark Tank India” has unveiled “#DontBeATota,” a campaign taking aim at founders who mistake the language of entrepreneurship for the substance of it, as the show opens its sixth season for entries on streamer SonyLIV.

	The campaign lands at a moment when terms such as valuation, burn rate and EBITDA have become everyday vocabulary. It poses a pointed question: has the language of entrepreneurship begun to overshadow the business itself? “#DontBeATota” argues that conviction, execution, resilience and a grasp of fundamentals matter more than a polished command of buzzwords.
	

	The campaign’s mascot is Tota, a parrot who believes he can impress the Sharks simply by reciting startup terminology he barely understands. The character is built around a simple contrast: anyone can memorize the vocabulary of entrepreneurship, but only founders who understand their business can actually build one.


	Aman Gupta, a Shark on “Shark Tank India” and co-founder of boAt and founder of Off/Beat, said: “If repeating startup buzzwords was enough to build a business, we would all be investing in parrots. That’s the thought behind this campaign. Over the years, ‘Shark Tank India’ has helped make entrepreneurship part of mainstream conversations, and that’s something we are incredibly proud of. But building a company has never been about sounding like an entrepreneur, it is about solving real problems, understanding your business inside out and showing the resilience to keep building every single day. That’s exactly the kind of founders we’re excited to meet in Season 6.”

	Founders can now submit entries for the new season through SonyLIV, with the network inviting applicants who can demonstrate real conviction and a clear understanding of their businesses. 
	

	The Script Room conceived and produced the campaign.

schema.headline: ‘Shark Tank India’ Sets Season 6 With Parrot Campaign on SonyLIV (EXCLUSIVE)

title: Korea Box Office: Na Hong-Jin’s ‘Hope’ Rules With 66% Market Share
description:
Local science-fiction survival thriller “Hope” stormed the top of the South Korean box office during the weekend of Jul. 17–19. 

	According to data
body:
Local science-fiction survival thriller “Hope” stormed the top of the South Korean box office during the weekend of Jul. 17–19. 

	According to data from KOBIS, the tracking service operated by the Korean Film Council, the highly anticipated blockbuster generated $11.3 million from 1,504,019 admissions over the three-day window, capturing a dominant 66.62% share of the total market revenue.


	Directed by Na Hong-Jin, the big-budget production features a star-studded international and local ensemble including Hwang Jung-min, Zo In-sung, Jung Ho-yeon, Michael Fassbender, and Alicia Vikander. The narrative centers on the residents of a remote, isolated harbor village who discover a mysterious alien entity on the outskirts of their town, quickly turning their quiet community into a desperate battleground for human survival. Capitalizing on massive pre-release anticipation and extensive preview screenings, the film has amassed a cumulative gross of $16 million from 2,129,723 total admissions.
	

	“Minions & Monsters” debuted in second place, capturing $2.1 million from 308,575 admissions over the frame. Since its launch, the film has gathered a total local cume of $2.6 million from 374,784 ticket buyers.


	“Moana” moved to third place during its sophomore outing, collecting $1.2 million from 173,555 admissions. The film has brought its South Korean cumulative haul to $5.6 million from 772,104 total viewers.

	“Toy Story 5” took fourth place, bringing in an additional $834,989 from 114,720 ticket buyers. The animation has pushed its total local footprint to $19.1 million from 2,750,183 total admissions since its mid-June premiere.
	

	Psychological horror-remake “The Eyes” finished in fifth place, generating $552,784 from 75,565 admissions. Directed by Yeom Ji-ho and starring Shin Min-ah and Kim Nam-hee, the thriller has maintained excellent longevity into its fourth frame, lifting its cumulative total to $9.9 million from 1,431,223 total admissions.

	Faith-based animated musical “David” was in sixth spot, pulling in $436,845 over the frame to push its overall total past the million-dollar threshold.

	Long-running genre juggernaut “Colony” placed seventh, adding $92,196 from 11,534 admissions. The Yeon Sang-ho viral hive-mind thriller starring Jun Ji-hyun, Koo Kyo-hwan, and Ji Chang-wook has achieved a cume of $42.2 million from 5,941,130 tickets since its May 21 arrival.

	The Japanese animated feature “Doraemon the Movie: New Nobita and the Castle of the Undersea Devil” followed in eighth place, pulling in $42,042 over the three-day weekend for a cumulative gross of $424,137.

	Josh Safdie’s table tennis comedy-drama “Marty Supreme,” starring Timothée Chalamet and Gwyneth Paltrow, took the ninth position with a weekend take of $48,551, bringing its cume to $853,810.

	Rounding out the bottom rung of the top 10 was the Japanese animated franchise entry “Bleach: Thousand-Year Blood War – The Calamity,” which added $30,669 from 3,492 admissions to push its cumulative total to $126,351.

	The overall market collective gross for the weekend was $17.1 million, a massive lift from last week’s $8.2 million.

schema.headline: Korea Box Office: Na Hong-Jin’s ‘Hope’ Rules With 66% Market Share

title: ‘Shrimad Bhagavatam’ Anchors Sagar Pictures Entertainment’s Global Push, Indian Studio Confirms (EXCLUSIVE)
description: Sagar Pictures Entertainment (SPE) has confirmed the launch of a global studio business, moving beyond film and television production into an intell
body:
Sagar Pictures Entertainment (SPE) has confirmed the launch of a global studio business, moving beyond film and television production into an intellectual property-led growth strategy spanning gaming, music, consumer products and theme parks.

	The Mumbai-based studio said it is transitioning from a traditional content company into what it describes as a future-focused entertainment enterprise, aiming to build franchises and original stories across multiple formats and platforms.
	

	“India is home to some of the world’s most timeless stories, many of which remain underexplored in cinema. These narratives are not only entertaining but also carry profound spiritual and cultural wisdom. At Sagar Pictures Entertainment, our vision is to preserve their authenticity while reimagining them through world-class cinematic storytelling for audiences across generations and geographies. This new chapter reflects our commitment to combining a seven-decade legacy with innovation, global collaborations, and future-ready entertainment experiences,” said Akash Sagar Chopra, CEO, Sagar Pictures Entertainment.


	“Our first step in this journey is ‘Shrimad Bhagavatam,’ a project that reflects the scale and ambition with which we want to approach storytelling. As we build this and future franchises, our focus is on creating stories that can grow beyond cinema into meaningful experiences across multiple platforms, while staying rooted in the authenticity and values that define India’s cultural heritage,” Chopra said.

	“Shrimad Bhagavatam” is SPE’s inaugural feature film franchise, conceived as a seven-chapter theatrical saga tailored for premium large-format screens. It is a live-action feature adaptation of the “Bhagavata Mahapurana,” one of Hinduism’s eighteen major Puranas centered on devotion to Krishna as an incarnation of Vishnu, alongside cosmology, genealogy and mythology. The franchise is currently in production and follows the studio’s earlier adaptations of Indian epics “Ramayana” and the “Mahabharata.”
	

	SPE builds on the legacy of the Sagar banner, founded in 1954 by filmmaker and producer Ramanand Sagar, a recipient of India’s Padma Shri honor. Under the guidance of chair and director Moti Sagar, the group has produced films and television programming known for adaptations of ancient Indian epics, positioning it as a name in spiritual, historical and family entertainment. The company’s productions have reached over a billion viewers in India, per BAARC India and other sources, with reach extending to the global Indian diaspora.

	SPE operates as the film studio arm of the Sagar Pix Group, based across Mumbai, Bengaluru and Hyderabad. Built on more than 75 years in storytelling, the studio has produced more than 55 television series and over 25 feature films for audiences in India, South Asia, the Middle East and North Africa (MENA) region and the global Indian diaspora. Its catalogue includes Indian National Film Award-winning features and award-winning television programming.

schema.headline: ‘Shrimad Bhagavatam’ Anchors Sagar Pictures Entertainment’s Global Push, Indian Studio Confirms (EXCLUSIVE)

title: ‘House of the Dragon’ Star Fabien Frankel on Criston Cole’s Suicide Mission and His ‘Nihilism’: ‘He’s Losing the Will to Motivate’
description:
SPOILER ALERT: This article contains spoilers for Season 3, Episode 5 of “House of the Dragon,” now streaming on HBO Max.

	In tonight’s “House of t
body:
SPOILER ALERT: This article contains spoilers for Season 3, Episode 5 of “House of the Dragon,” now streaming on HBO Max.

	In tonight’s “House of the Dragon” episode, Ser Criston Cole (Fabien Frankel) proclaims that he’s ready to die — and he’s ready to do so honorably.

	As the war to capture the Iron Throne rages on, Criston marches onward with his men, including Gwayne Hightower (Freddie Fox), to put a stop to the Rivermen reaching King’s Landing and aiding Rhaenyra Targaryen (Emma D’Arcy). But their journey to catch up with the Rivermen proves increasingly difficult, after one of their horses gets caught in a trap. Once they come across a burned-down bridge to cross the river, they realize there is no chance of catching up.
	

	Gwayne and Criston regroup and debate what to do next, now down to half the men they started with. Gwayne says continuing forward “would march us toward certain death.” That’s when Gwayne realizes this was Criston’s goal all along, as Criston launches into a revealing monologue: “I have been a servant all my life. To Green or to Black. I have defined myself by oaths, through unyielding devotion. And the highborn have made much of me while it pleased them to do so. But I will not let them diminish me now. I will not return spurned, rejected. I am the realm’s greatest warrior. And I will die as such.”


	While Frankel says he doesn’t read fan reactions much these days, it has become easier to tap into his controversial character’s motivations in Season 3: “I can only understand why people feel how they feel about him as a character. It’s tricky for me as an actor, because obviously, I just don’t see him in that way. It serves no purpose for me to see him in that way. You always have to be the bastion of your character, and I feel very much the bastion of Criston Cole.”
	

	Frankel spoke with Variety to break down Criston’s suicide mission, why being a soldier is “the only honest version of himself,” and what fans can expect — should the series continue to follow George R.R. Martin’s book “A Song of Ice and Fire,” as expected — from the upcoming Butcher’s Ball.
	
		This is a pivotal moment for Criston, proclaiming that he is ready to die an honorable death. Can you talk about his journey throughout this episode, and how he arrives at this point?	

	It’s something that’s been brewing inside of him so much over the course of the show, probably since about halfway through Season 2. These thoughts of, “What am I going to be, and what’s the legacy I’m going to leave behind? In what way do I want to go out?” I suppose that really comes to a head in that fire scene with Gwayne.
	
		Does he have a specific vision for how he wants his death to play out? How can he guarantee he’s going to go out the way he wants, and that people will remember him?	

	I don’t know if there’s anything in his head — it’s like he said, “Hell bent in glory,” right? Criston Cole started as a soldier before the 20-year stretch that he spent at King’s Landing — that’s how he started, and that’s how he wants to finish. In his mind, the only honest version of himself is the soldier. Everything else is pretense; the diplomat is pretense; the Hand of the King, there’s a level of pretense; Lord Commander of the Kingsguard, there’s a level of pretense. I suppose they’re all psychological, brain-led jobs. He isn’t that way, he’s very much led by his sword and his fighting instinct. The idea that that’s how he will be remembered, I think, feels honest.
	
		Gwayne eventually figures out what’s really going on. Criston is very upfront about his suicide mission once it clicks.	

	There’s a genuine fondness that Criston has for Gwayne; there’s a yin-yang aspect to them. Weirdly, I think Gwayne represents — on the surface — everything that Criston disdains in this world. Yet underneath, Criston’s found someone who will go to battle with him, who does have a heart, who does have empathy, who is much more than he appears. So their relationship is built through this, these guys going to war with each other. You hear any soldier talk about their time in the war, in any war, and they’ll say the relationships they formed are closer than anything you can possibly imagine because you’re living through life and death with these people. You’re risking your lives for each other. As a result of that, there’s a profoundness to their relationship.
	



	

									Theo Whiteman/HBO
	


	
		Why does Criston, then, decide not to tell Gwayne about his suicide mission for this long?	

	I don’t think he’s waiting for Gwayne to figure it out, it’s not a game. Certainly, I didn’t think of it as a game. I think he just knew that as soon as he told Gwayne, that would create a lack of desire to continue. Gwayne’s dislike of this plan would rub off on the rest of the men, and then Criston would lose an army that otherwise would have a good chance of fighting. There’s undeniably a selfishness to Criston’s plan, right? He’s looking out for himself but I think he’s become so accustomed to a world where every single person is looking out for themselves constantly. Unfortunately, any sort of attempt at selflessness falls on itself. For any of the characters, it’s just a very dog-eat-dog world.
	
		You’ve spoken in previous interviews about not always knowing Criston’s code, or where his motivations lie episode to episode. As the season has continued on, have you been able to tap into these more?	

	There’s a freedom that comes with nihilism, to a degree. This is a character who, at least at the top of Season 2 and toward the end of Season 3, surrendered himself to the notion that the war is over. That ultimately, we as human beings have no genuinely important part to play in a war that’s ultimately going to be won by dragons or lost by dragons. They are just so much more powerful, and so you meet him at the top of Season 3 in a genuinely kind of — I wouldn’t say nihilistic, because I think that’s a bit reductive. But certainly, there’s an agnosticism to him. He’s losing the will to motivate. He finds that will as the course of the season goes on, and certainly in Episode 4’s planning scene with Gwayne that really gives him the impetus. It re-energizes him and then he certainly has rediscovered his sense of code — not code so much, but a sense of purpose.
	
		This episode comes on the heels of Criston finding out that Aemond (Ewan Mitchell) has left him behind, and he’s once again hit with those feelings of abandonment that he’s felt all his life. How does that play into his actions here?	

	It’s tricky, man. I want to think there’s a part of him that is intelligent enough — not intelligent enough — but a part of him that certainly recognizes who Aemond is. I just don’t believe it’s all that surprising that Aemond hasn’t shown, even if he’s playing. Sometimes in order to sell a story as people, we go along with notions that feel like the most natural ones. So Criston saying, “He wouldn’t desert us, I can’t believe that,” I almost feel like that’s him playing into the version of this character that needs that piece of information in order to rediscover a sense of self and purpose.
	
	
		How does he look back on his relationships with Rhaenyra and Alicent Hightower (Olivia Cooke) now — does he have regrets? As he’s spending these long periods traveling to battle, ready to fall on his sword, is he reflecting on his decisions, or is he just focused on charging forward?	

	Haven’t men always done that in history? Just march on, and go to work, “I’m not going to deal with anything that I’m really feeling, I’m just going to go on and get on and do something, and that thing is going to supersede whatever I’m feeling!” But it’s inevitable that Rhaenyra and Alicent are both on his mind all the time. I think Alicent much more than Rhaenyra. Certainly for me, Rhaenyra has become a sort of afterthought. And actually, it’s just the idea of winning the war for Alicent.
	
		Has it become easier to tap into Criston’s humanity, despite him being such a controversial character?	

	There’s just genuine insight into who this man is, and what makes him tick. Yeah, I think I have found it easier to tap into the humanity of him. It’s difficult, you can’t undo his actions. He’s done the things that he’s done, and those things have been, at times, very morally reprehensible or questionable, to say the least. And so I can only understand why people feel how they feel about him as a character. It’s tricky for me as an actor because obviously, I just don’t see him in that way. It serves no purpose for me to see him in that way. You always have to be the bastion of your character, and I feel very much the bastion of Criston Cole.
	
		How do you look back on your journey on the show and playing Criston?	

	I learned so much from playing Criston Cole. There’s a stillness to him that I don’t naturally have as a person, and I think I’ve learned so much through that. We’re so different as people, but I look back on the last few years with just a ton of warmth towards the people I’ve gotten to work with. Irrespective of playing the character or making the show, it’s the company that you keep that makes any experience.
	
		Your former “House of the Dragon” co-star, Milly Alcock, is having a big summer with “Supergirl.” Have you had a chance to see the film, do you keep in touch with Milly at all?	

	I keep in touch with her all the time, yeah! I spoke to her on the phone two weeks ago when she called me from Brazil — I think she was doing press. I’m immensely proud of her, and not in the least bit surprised. I told my agent he should sign her as soon as we did Season 1 of “House of the Dragon” together, and that’s what he did. I’m so happy for her, very proud of her. I haven’t yet had a chance to see “Supergirl,” but I will!
	
	
		I know fans of the book will be looking forward to Butcher’s Ball, which the end of this week’s episode seems to set up. What can you say about the action scenes coming up?	

	All I can say is that it was long and arduous choreography to learn, it’s a brilliantly choreographed fight. There are a ton of unbelievable stuntmen in it that make the fight what it is, and I’m just super excited for people to see that side of Criston. It’s the side of him we haven’t really seen at all — what he is like in a man-to-man situation.

	This interview has been edited and condensed.

schema.headline: ‘House of the Dragon’ Star Fabien Frankel on Criston Cole’s Suicide Mission and His ‘Nihilism’: ‘He’s Losing the Will to Motivate’

title: J.D. Vance and Wife Usha Welcome Baby Boy, Make Vice Presidential History
description:
J.D. And Usha Vance
                          Welcome to the Family, Baby Alec!!!
body:
J.D. And Usha Vance
                          Welcome to the Family, Baby Alec!!!
                      
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            Vice President J.D. Vance and Second Lady Usha Vance just added another little Vance to the family ... welcoming their fourth child and making a little history in the process.
      
    
            The couple announced Sunday their baby boy, Alec Neel Vance, was born earlier in the morning ... saying both mom and Alec are happy and healthy, while their three older children are "overjoyed" to meet their new brother.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            The birth is also one for the history books -- Alec is reportedly the first child born to a sitting U.S. vice president in more than 150 years. The last was Schuyler Colfax III, whose father was serving as VP when he arrived in 1870.
      

            Alec joins siblings Ewan, 9, Vivek, 6, and Mirabel, 4 ... giving the vice president a full house at the Naval Observatory.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            J.D. and Usha thanked the doctors and staff at Walter Reed National Military Medical Center, along with the White House medical team, for caring for their growing family.
      
    
            The new arrival fits right in with J.D.'s outspoken push for Americans to have more kids ... he declared at the 2025 March for Life rally, "I want more babies in the United States of America."
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            Usha announced her pregnancy in January and later got plenty of attention for rocking an $8.75 Old Navy maternity dress -- prompting J.D. to jokingly nominate his bargain-hunting wife as the country's next budget director.
      
    
            The VP has wanted more babies in America -- and the Vances are leading by example.
      
      
              
          
                          
                J.D. Vance
              
                          
                Family
              
                          
                White House
              
                          
                Politics
              
                          
                News

schema.headline: J.D. Vance and Wife Usha Welcome Baby Boy, Make Vice Presidential History

title: Celebrities Eating Ice Cream For National Ice Cream Day

                          Celebs Scream for Ice Cream!

National Ice Cream Day
                          Celebs Scream for Ice Cream!
                      
      
    
      
        
              
    
    
            It's National Ice Cream Day ... and you're not the only one screaming for ice cream ... Hollywood is joining the chorus too!!!
      
    
            That's because even A-listers aren't immune to a little sweet treat, especially as temps pick up this summer.
      
    
            Celebs like Sydney Sweeney and Ryan Seacrest have been snapped indulging in a little ice cream. Tyra Banks couldn't be beat, though ... triple-fisting three cones at once!
      
    
            But they aren't the only stars who love satisfying their sweet tooth ... check out our gallery to see all the ice cream lovers!

                          'Memba Her?!
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              July 20, 2026
                            12:01 AM PDT
                                                                            
      
    
            Actress Nathalia Ramos was in her late teens when she first started playing Nina Martin -- who moves to a mysterious English boarding school called "Anubis House" after receiving a scholarship -- on "House of Anubis" back in 2011.
      
    
            Joining Nathalia on set included Jade Ramsey, Brad Kavanagh, and Tasie Lawrence.
      
    
            Guess what she looks like now!

title: Lady Gaga Celebrates Manager Bobby Campbell's Wedding With Fiancé
description:
Lady Gaga & Michael Polansky
                          Date Night for Her Manager's Big 'I Do'
body:
Lady Gaga & Michael Polansky
                          Date Night for Her Manager's Big 'I Do'
                      
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            Lady Gaga was in celebration mode this weekend ... 'cause TMZ has learned she and her fiancé, Michael Polansky, stepped out for date night at the wedding of her longtime manager and close friend, Bobby Campbell, and his husband.
      
    
            Sources with direct knowledge tell TMZ ... Gaga and Polansky attended the Saturday night ceremony at the Walt Disney Concert Hall in downtown Los Angeles, where Campbell and Robert García tied the knot surrounded by family and friends.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            We're told Gaga kept a low profile throughout the evening, blending in with the rest of the guests instead of stealing the spotlight. And despite her superstar status, our sources say she did not perform during the reception.
      

            Instead, we're told "Gypsy Woman" singer Crystal Waters provided the night's entertainment, getting guests on their feet during the celebration.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            Campbell and Gaga go way back. He's managed the pop icon for years and has helped steer some of the biggest moments of her career, from chart-topping albums and global tours to her Oscar-winning era and recent projects.
      
    
            Their relationship has long extended beyond business, with Gaga frequently praising Campbell publicly and the two sharing a close friendship.
      
      
              
          
                          
                Michael Polansky
              
                          
                Lady Gaga

schema.headline: Lady Gaga Celebrates Manager Bobby Campbell's Wedding With Fiancé
